## src/content/blog/accessible-forms.mdx

```md
---
title: 'Building Forms Everyone Can Use'
description: 'Practical accessibility patterns for forms — validation messaging, error grouping, focus management, and screen reader support.'
pubDate: '2022-08-12T00:00:00-04:00'
tags: ['accessibility', 'engineering']
heroImage: '@images/accessible-forms-hero.webp'
---

Forms are the most common interaction on the web, and they're also where accessibility failures cluster. The good news is that fixing them doesn't require heroics — just attention to a few patterns.

## Label Everything

Every input needs a `<label>`. Placeholders are not labels — they disappear on input, leaving users with no context.

```html
<label for="email">Email address</label>
<input type="email" id="email" name="email" autocomplete="email" />
```

## Error Messages That Work

Inline validation is best, but it needs to be connected to its input programmatically:

```html
<label for="name">Full name</label>
<input
  type="text"
  id="name"
  name="name"
  aria-describedby="name-error"
  aria-invalid="true"
/>
<span id="name-error" role="alert">Name is required.</span>
```

`aria-describedby` ties the error to the input so screen readers announce it when focusing. `role="alert"` makes the error message interrupt politely.

## Focus Management After Submission

After a form submission with errors, move focus to the first invalid input. This prevents keyboard users from having to tab through the entire form again to find what went wrong.

```js
const firstError = form.querySelector('[aria-invalid="true"]');
if (firstError) firstError.focus();
```

## Testing

Run your form through axe DevTools, then navigate it with a keyboard. If you can complete the flow with only Tab, Enter, and Escape, you're 90% of the way there.

```

---

## src/content/blog/astro-view-transitions.mdx

```md
---
title: 'Astro View Transitions: A Practical Walkthrough'
description: 'How Astros view transition API works under the hood — morphing, naming, and avoiding common pitfalls with client-side state.'
pubDate: '2022-08-19T00:00:00-04:00'
tags: ['astro']
heroImage: '@images/astro-view-transitions-hero.webp'
---

Astro's View Transitions API brings native-like page transitions to multi-page applications without a heavy client-side framework. It's built on the browser's View Transitions API with Astro-specific enhancements.

## Getting Started

Add the component to your layout head:

```astro
---
import { ViewTransitions } from 'astro:transitions';
---
<html>
  <head>
    <ViewTransitions />
  </head>
</html>
```

That's it. Every navigation between pages now transitions with a cross-fade by default.

## Named Elements for Morphing

The real power comes from `transition:name` and `transition:animate`. Elements with the same name across pages morph smoothly:

```astro
<!-- On the blog index -->
<img src={post.image} alt={post.title} transition:name={post.slug} />

<!-- On the individual post page -->
<img src={post.image} alt={post.title} transition:name={post.slug} transition:animate="morph" />
```

The image scales and morphs between its position on the index and its position on the detail page. No JS required.

## Preserving Client State

Frameworks like React or Alpine maintain their state across navigations if you use `transition:persist`:

```astro
<Header transition:persist />
```

The header DOM element is reused across navigations, so open menus, scroll position, and hover states survive.

## Caveats

View transitions require the navigation to be same-origin. External links and form submissions aren't intercepted. Also, the animations run on the compositor thread, so they won't block interaction — but they can look janky if the pages have very different layouts. Use `transition:animate="morph"` selectively.

```

---

## src/content/blog/building-component-library.mdx

```md
---
title: 'Building a Component Library From Scratch'
description: 'Framework choice, API design, testing strategy, and documentation decisions for a component library that teams actually adopt.'
pubDate: '2022-09-30T00:00:00-04:00'
tags: ['engineering', 'design-systems', 'react']
heroImage: '@images/building-component-library-hero.webp'
---

Starting a component library is deceptively hard. The first few components are easy. The 50th — when you're dealing with composition, theming, and cross-team API preferences — is where most libraries fall apart.

## Framework Choice

If your organization uses one framework, build for it. If it uses multiple, build with web components or use a framework-agnostic approach with a thin adapter layer. Radix UI and Ariakit provide headless primitives that you style, which is usually a better investment than building from zero.

## API Design Principles

- Minimize required props. If a good default exists, use it.
- Spread remaining props onto the root DOM element so consumers can override anything.
- Expose `asChild` polymorphic patterns for layout flexibility.

```tsx
type Props = React.ComponentPropsWithoutRef<'button'> & {
  variant?: 'primary' | 'secondary';
  loading?: boolean;
};

function Button({ variant = 'primary', loading, children, ...props }: Props) {
  return (
    <button className={`btn btn--${variant}`} disabled={loading} {...props}>
      {loading ? <Spinner /> : children}
    </button>
  );
}
```

## Documentation

Storybook is the standard for a reason. Every component should have stories for its default state, every variant, error state, loading state, and edge case. If it's not documented, it might as well not exist.

## Testing

Test behavior, not implementation. A button test should verify that `onClick` fires, not that a specific CSS class is present. Use Testing Library for React components and Playwright for integration tests that verify real browser behavior.

```

---

## src/content/blog/choosing-a-color-palette.mdx

```md
---
title: 'Choosing a Color Palette: From Cold Blue to Warm Amber'
description: 'Why I moved this blog from a default electric blue palette to a warm amber/coral editorial scheme — and how OKLCH made it painless.'
pubDate: '2026-06-16T00:00:00-04:00'
tags: ['css', 'design-systems']
heroImage: '@images/choosing-a-color-palette-hero.webp'
---

Most developer blogs ship with a cold color palette. Electric blues, teals, cyber-greens — the default "tech" look. When I rebuilt this site on Astro, it was no different. The original palette centered on `oklch(0.6 0.22 260)` — a punchy blue accent against deep navy-ish surfaces.

It looked fine. It was not wrong. But it felt like every other dev blog.

## What was wrong with blue

The original palette was serviceable, but it had two problems:

**Every tech blog uses blue.** Look at the default themes for Astro, Next.js, Vercel, GitHub — they all lean cool. Standing out is hard when you're using the same hue as everyone else.

**Cold light, cold dark.** Blue-based dark themes can feel clinical. The goal of this site is to be warm and approachable — a place where I write about design systems and accessibility with some humanity. The blue was fighting that.

## The case for amber

I spent an afternoon in the OKLCH color picker, rotating the hue and watching the feel change.

Landing on `oklch(0.7 0.18 70)` — a warm amber — was immediate. The dark mode got richer. Text popped differently. The whole thing felt less like a dashboard and more like a publication.

The palette settled into three accents:

- **Brand primary** (`oklch(0.7 0.18 70)`): Used for links, hover states, borders on card hover. A warm amber that reads as energetic but not aggressive.
- **Brand accent** (`oklch(0.65 0.2 30)`): A coral-sienna for secondary interactions and focus outlines. Warmer than the primary.
- **Brand highlight** (`oklch(0.85 0.14 90)`): A soft gold for link hover states against the dark background.

The background shifted from blue-tinted charcoal (`oklch(0.12 0.015 260)`) to a warmer near-black (`oklch(0.12 0.015 280)`) — just a 20-degree rotation, but it removed the cold cast.

## The light theme

Light mode inverted cleanly. Same hues, just shifted lightness and reduced chroma to keep contrast ratios accessible. The amber becomes a slightly desaturated `oklch(0.48 0.2 70)` — still warm, still amber, but comfortable on white.

The light theme is opt-in only — no `prefers-color-scheme` media query. Readers who prefer dark stay in dark. This avoids the whiplash of a site flipping modes based on system settings.

## What OKLCH gave us

Using OKLCH for the entire palette was the right call for three reasons:

1. **Hue-independent lightness.** You can rotate hue without distorting perceived brightness. Try that in HSL and you get muddy desaturated nonsense.
2. **Single-source theming.** Light and dark themes are the same OKLCH values with different `l` and `c` parameters. Nothing else changes.
3. **Future-proof gamut.** OKLCH covers P3 and Rec.2020. When displays catch up, the colors will just look better without touching the code.

## The outcome

After the switch, Lighthouse scores stayed at 100/100 across all categories. The design reads warmer, more editorial, and less like a template. The amber feels intentional — like the blog was built around it, not painted over it.

If you are building a personal site, spend an hour in OKLCH before you settle on your palette. The difference between a default palette and a chosen one is everything.

```

---

## src/content/blog/core-web-vitals.mdx

```md
---
title: 'Core Web Vitals: Beyond the Dashboard'
description: 'Debugging LCP, CLS, and INP in production — real causes, diagnostic tools, and fixes that actually move the percentile needle.'
pubDate: '2022-09-09T00:00:00-04:00'
tags: ['performance']
heroImage: '@images/core-web-vitals-hero.webp'
---

The Core Web Vitals dashboard tells you what's slow, but not why. Bridging that gap requires understanding what each metric actually measures and how the browser computes it.

## LCP: Largest Contentful Paint

The most common culprit is the hero image. But not always — LCP can also be a text block rendered with a web font that loads late.

For images, preload the LCP candidate:

```html
<link rel="preload" as="image" href="/hero.webp" fetchpriority="high" />
```

For text, ensure your font is self-hosted, preloaded, and uses `font-display: swap` with a matching fallback.

## CLS: Cumulative Layout Shift

CLS happens when elements move after the user has started interacting. The most common sources:

- Images without dimensions — always set width and height
- Ads and embeds that reserve no space
- Web fonts causing a text re-layout (FOUT/FOIT)

The fix for images is simple and has been standard advice for years: always set `width` and `height` on images, or use `aspect-ratio` in CSS.

## INP: Interaction to Next Frame

INP measures the delay between a user interaction and the next frame being painted. Long tasks are the enemy here. Break up JavaScript-heavy interactions with `setTimeout` or `scheduler.yield()` to let the browser paint between chunks.

## Measuring in the Field

Lab data (Lighthouse) catches regressions. Field data (CrUX, RUM) catches real user pain. You need both. A fast lab score with poor field data means your testing doesn't match your users' reality — usually slower devices or networks you're not emulating.

```

---

## src/content/blog/css-container-queries.mdx

```md
---
title: 'CSS Container Queries in Practice'
description: 'Beyond the basics — real patterns for container query units, nested contexts, and progressive enhancement with fallbacks.'
pubDate: '2022-08-05T00:00:00-04:00'
tags: ['css']
heroImage: '@images/css-container-queries-hero.webp'
---

Container queries are the most important CSS feature since Grid. They let components respond to their own size rather than the viewport, which is what we actually wanted all along.

## The Mental Shift

With media queries, you think in terms of page layouts. With container queries, you think in terms of component layouts. A card component styles itself differently depending on how much room it has, regardless of whether it's in a sidebar or a full-width section.

```css
.card-wrapper {
  container-type: inline-size;
  container-name: card;
}

@container card (min-width: 400px) {
  .card {
    display: grid;
    grid-template-columns: 200px 1fr;
  }
}
```

## Container Query Units

cqw, cqh, cqi, cqmin, cqmax — these are relative to the container rather than the viewport. They're incredibly useful for typography that scales with its container:

```css
.card-title {
  font-size: clamp(1rem, 4cqi, 2rem);
}
```

## Browser Support and Fallbacks

Container queries are well-supported now, but if you need to support older browsers, use a `@supports` query:

```css
.card {
  /* Fallback: stacked layout */
  display: block;
}

@supports (container-type: inline-size) {
  .card-wrapper {
    container-type: inline-size;
  }

  @container (min-width: 400px) {
    .card {
      display: grid;
      grid-template-columns: 200px 1fr;
    }
  }
}
```

The fallback is just a simpler layout. No harm done.

```

---

## src/content/blog/css-grid-layouts.mdx

```md
---
title: 'CSS Grid Layouts Beyond the Basics'
description: 'Subgrid, auto-fill vs auto-fit, grid areas for responsive dashboards, and when not to use Grid at all.'
pubDate: '2022-10-14T00:00:00-04:00'
tags: ['css']
heroImage: '@images/css-grid-layouts-hero.webp'
---

CSS Grid is the most powerful layout tool in browsers, but most usage stays at the surface level — three-column layouts with equal gaps. The real power comes from the features most people skip.

## Subgrid

Subgrid lets child elements align to the parent grid rather than defining their own. This is essential for card layouts where the content varies:

```css
.card-list {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(300px, 1fr));
  grid-template-rows: auto 1fr auto;
}

.card {
  display: grid;
  grid-row: span 3;
  grid-template-rows: subgrid;
}
```

Now every card's header, body, and footer align across the entire row, regardless of content length.

## Auto-fill vs Auto-fit

The difference is subtle and often misunderstood:

- `auto-fill` creates empty tracks when there aren't enough items to fill a row
- `auto-fit` collapses empty tracks so items expand to fill available space

Use `auto-fit` when you want items to grow. Use `auto-fill` when you want consistent track sizing.

## When Not to Use Grid

Grid is not a replacement for Flexbox. Flexbox is for one-dimensional layouts where items wrap. Grid is for two-dimensional layouts where you need alignment across both axes. If you only need to space items in a row with wrapping, use Flexbox. The code will be simpler and more predictable.

```

---

## src/content/blog/design-token-workflows.mdx

```md
---
title: 'Design Token Workflows at Scale'
description: 'Managing color, spacing, and typography tokens across multiple products — versioning, distribution, and naming conventions that survive.'
pubDate: '2022-09-02T00:00:00-04:00'
tags: ['design-systems', 'css']
heroImage: '@images/design-token-workflows-hero.webp'
---

Design tokens are the atoms of a design system. When they're well-structured, changing the global border radius or primary color is a one-line PR. When they're not, you're hunting through CSS files at 2 AM.

## Naming Conventions That Scale

The most durable naming system follows a category-concept-modifier pattern:

```
--color-surface-primary
--color-surface-elevated
--space-inline-sm
--space-inline-lg
```

Avoid naming by visual effect (`--color-shadow-light`) or by use case (`--sidebar-bg`). The first leaks implementation details and the second creates a new token every time a component needs its own color.

## Distribution

For a single project, CSS custom properties in a central file work fine. For multi-product systems, you need a package:

```
@mycompany/tokens
  /tokens
    color.json
    spacing.json
    typography.json
  /dist
    tokens.css
    tokens.esm.js
```

Build the dist from source JSON so design tools and developers consume the same values. Style Dictionary handles this transformation reliably.

## Versioning

Tokens are a public API. Breaking changes — renaming a token, removing one, changing its value in a way that breaks a component's visual contract — require a major version bump. Additive changes are minor. Documentation-only changes are patch.

This discipline forces teams to think before renaming things, which is precisely the friction you want.

```

---

## src/content/blog/design-tokens.mdx

```md
---
title: 'Design Tokens on This Site'
description: 'A reference for the CSS custom properties that power color, typography, spacing, and layout — how they work together and why they are set up this way.'
pubDate: 'Jun 16 2026'
tags: ['css', 'design-systems']
heroImage: '@images/design-tokens-hero.webp'
---

Every visual decision on this site flows through CSS custom properties defined in a single file: `src/styles/global.css`. This article documents those tokens, their relationships, and the rationale behind them.

## Typography

### Font Families

```css
--font-copy: "Inter Variable", system-ui, sans-serif;
--font-headers: "Plus Jakarta Sans Variable", system-ui, sans-serif;
--font-mono: "Fira Code Variable", ui-monospace, monospace;
```

Inter is the workhorse body face — narrow, tall x-height, excellent at small sizes. Plus Jakarta Sans handles headings with a more geometric, open character. Fira Code provides monospace with ligatures.

### Type Size Scale

Every size is fluid — it scales smoothly between viewport widths using `clamp()`:

```css
--type-size-body:   clamp(1rem,    0.45vw + 0.91rem, 1.25rem);   /* 16px → 20px */
--type-size-small:  clamp(0.8125rem, 0.2vw + 0.77rem, 0.9375rem); /* 13px → 15px */
--type-size-h1:     clamp(2rem,    3.64vw + 1.27rem,  4rem);      /* 32px → 64px */
--type-size-h2:     clamp(1.625rem, 2.13vw + 1.2rem, 2.8rem);    /* 26px → 45px */
--type-size-h3:     clamp(1.375rem, 1.14vw + 1.15rem, 2rem);     /* 22px → 32px */
--type-size-h4:     clamp(1.125rem, 0.57vw + 1.07rem, 1.5rem);   /* 18px → 24px */
--type-size-h5:     clamp(1rem,    0.28vw + 0.94rem, 1.1875rem); /* 16px → 19px */
--type-size-blockquote: 1.1em;
```

The scale uses a roughly 1.25 modular ratio (major third). The `clamp()` endpoints are chosen so that body text never drops below 16 px (the minimum for comfortable long-form reading on a 13″ laptop) and never exceeds it by more than 25 %.

### Line Heights

```css
--line-height-body: 1.65;    /* body copy — generous for readability */
```

There is no separate heading line-height token. Each heading level sets its own line-height as a multiple of the vertical rhythm unit (see below).

## Vertical Rhythm

The entire vertical spacing system is anchored to one value:

```css
--rhythm: calc(var(--line-height-body) * 1rem);  /* ≈ 1.65 rem */
```

`--rhythm` equals one line of body text. Every margin, padding, and heading line-height is a clean multiple of this unit, creating a consistent baseline grid.

### Heading Sizes and Spacing

| Heading | Font size (fluid) | Line-height (× rhythm) | Top margin | Bottom margin |
|---------|-------------------|------------------------|------------|---------------|
| h1      | 32 → 64 px        | 3× (≈ 79 px)           | 0          | 1× (≈ 26 px)  |
| h2      | 26 → 45 px        | 2× (≈ 53 px)           | 2×         | 1×            |
| h3      | 22 → 32 px        | 2× (≈ 53 px)           | 1.5×       | 0.5×          |
| h4      | 18 → 24 px        | 1.5× (≈ 40 px)         | 1.5×       | 0.5×          |
| h5      | 16 → 19 px        | 1.25× (≈ 33 px)        | 1×         | 0.5×          |

The bottom margin of a heading is always smaller than its top margin, so each heading visually attaches to the content below it rather than floating between sections.

## Spacing Scale

The spacing tokens provide the building blocks for layout, padding, and gaps. Every value is a multiple of `--rhythm`:

```css
--space-xs: calc(var(--rhythm) * 0.5);   /*  0.5× ≈ 13 px */
--space-sm: calc(var(--rhythm) * 0.75);  /*  0.75× ≈ 20 px */
--space-md: calc(var(--rhythm) * 1);     /*  1×   ≈ 26 px */
--space-lg: calc(var(--rhythm) * 1.5);   /*  1.5× ≈ 40 px */
--space-xl: calc(var(--rhythm) * 2);     /*  2×   ≈ 53 px */
```

These are further aliased by purpose so that changing one token adjusts spacing everywhere it is used:

```css
--space-inline: var(--space-xs);            /* tight inline gaps (tag spacing, icon gaps) */
--space-inline-strong: var(--space-sm);      /* stronger inline separation (card children) */
--space-component: var(--space-md);          /* default component padding */
--space-component-lg: var(--space-lg);        /* large component padding */
--space-section: var(--space-lg);            /* section-level margins (headings, blockquotes) */
```

## Color Palette

The colors use the OKLCH color space for perceptual uniformity. The default theme is a warm dark mode with an amber/coral editorial palette.

Both override mechanisms are supported: the `[data-theme="light"]` attribute selector for the JS theme toggle, and `@media (prefers-color-scheme: light)` for system-level preference. They hold identical values, so the light appearance is the same regardless of which trigger fires.

### Dark Theme (default)

```css
--bg-main: oklch(0.12 0.015 280);             /* deep navy-black background */
--bg-surface: oklch(0.16 0.02 280);           /* card / surface background */
--bg-surface-elevated: oklch(0.2 0.025 280);  /* elevated surface (hovered cards) */

--text-primary: oklch(0.95 0.008 280);        /* main body text — 15.05:1 contrast */
--text-secondary: oklch(0.77 0.015 280);      /* secondary text — 4.50:1 contrast */
--text-muted: oklch(0.72 0.02 280);           /* muted / meta text — 3.28:1 contrast */

--brand-primary: oklch(0.64 0.16 75);         /* Primary Amber */
--brand-accent: oklch(0.58 0.18 35);          /* Accent Coral */
--brand-highlight: oklch(0.81 0.15 110);      /* Highlight Chartreuse */
--neutral-anchor: oklch(0.53 0.08 230);       /* Anchor Slate */
--accent-cold: oklch(0.69 0.15 250);          /* Interaction Cerulean */
```

### Light Theme Override

The light theme is triggered by either the attribute selector or the system preference media query, and overrides only the color tokens. Typography and spacing stay the same:

```css
[data-theme="light"] {
  --bg-main: oklch(0.985 0.003 280);           /* near-white background */
  --bg-surface: oklch(1 0 0);                  /* pure white cards */
  --bg-surface-elevated: oklch(0.92 0.008 280);

  --text-primary: oklch(0.13 0.015 280);
  --text-secondary: oklch(0.25 0.02 280);
  --text-muted: oklch(0.38 0.025 280);

  --brand-primary: oklch(0.55 0.15 75);        /* Rich Amber */
  --brand-accent: oklch(0.48 0.16 35);         /* Deep Coral */
  --brand-highlight: oklch(0.45 0.14 110);     /* High-Contrast Chartreuse */
  --neutral-anchor: oklch(0.35 0.06 230);      /* Dark Slate Anchor */
  --accent-cold: oklch(0.52 0.14 250);         /* Deep Interactive Cerulean */
}

@media (prefers-color-scheme: light) {
  :root { /* identical values */ }
}
```

Using OKLCH ensures that the light and dark themes stay perceptually consistent — the same hue angles produce recognizably the same color families across themes, differing only in lightness. Every text token on both themes exceeds WCAG AA contrast against its background.

## Border Radii

```css
--radius-sm: 4px;
--radius-md: 8px;
--radius-lg: 12px;
```

A tight radius system. Cards use `--radius-lg`, buttons and inputs use `--radius-md`, code blocks use `--radius-sm`.

## Motion

```css
--transition-fast: 0.15s ease-out;
--transition-normal: 0.25s ease-out;
--transition-slow: 0.4s ease-out;
```

All transitions use `ease-out` — fast in, slow out — which feels responsive because the motion starts immediately and coasts to rest. The `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce` media query disables all transitions and animations.

## Structural Tokens

```css
--size-border-pixel: 2px;
--size-border-thick: 4px;
--size-touch-target-min: 44px;

--layout-max-width: 68ch;           /* comfortable reading measure */
--layout-max-width-wide: 75rem;     /* full page content area */
--bp-mobile: 720px;                 /* mobile breakpoint */
```

The 68-character line length for body text follows the established range of 45–75 characters per line. The touch target minimum of 44px matches [WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 2.5.8](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/#target-size).

```

---

## src/content/blog/error-monitoring.mdx

```md
---
title: 'Setting Up Error Monitoring for Your Frontend'
description: 'Source maps, grouping strategies, breadcrumbs, and keeping noise low so you actually look at the errors that matter.'
pubDate: '2022-11-04T00:00:00-04:00'
tags: ['engineering']
heroImage: '@images/error-monitoring-hero.webp'
---

Error monitoring tells you what broke for real users in production. Without it, you're debugging blind — relying on users to file bug reports that usually say "it didn't work" with no reproduction steps.

## Choosing a Service

Sentry is the standard for frontend error tracking. It groups errors intelligently, captures context (browser, OS, URL, previous actions), and supports source maps for readable stack traces. OpenReplay adds session replays if you need to see what the user was doing before the error occurred.

## Source Maps Are Non-Negotiable

Minified stack traces are useless. Upload source maps to your error tracker as part of your build pipeline:

```bash
sentry-cli releases files $VERSION upload-sourcemaps ./dist
```

Make sure source maps are not publicly accessible — Sentry uses authentication, but publicly hosted source maps expose your source code to anyone who inspects the network tab.

## Breadcrumbs for Context

Configure breadcrumbs to capture meaningful user actions before the error:

```js
Sentry.addBreadcrumb({
  category: 'navigation',
  message: 'Navigated to /settings',
  level: 'info',
});
```

When an error occurs, the breadcrumb trail tells you what the user was doing, which is usually more useful than the error message itself.

## Noise Reduction

Not every error needs a notification. Ignore known non-issues — `ResizeObserver loop limit exceeded`, `Script error` (cross-origin), and errors from browser extensions. Set a reasonable rate limit for the same error to avoid alert fatigue.

Aim for a signal-to-noise ratio where every alert prompts action. If you're ignoring more than you're reading, tighten your filters.

```

---

## src/content/blog/image-optimization.mdx

```md
---
title: 'Image Optimization for the Modern Web'
description: 'AVIF, WebP, responsive srcset, lazy loading, and how to build an image pipeline that automates all of it.'
pubDate: '2022-10-28T00:00:00-04:00'
tags: ['performance']
heroImage: '@images/image-optimization-hero.webp'
---

Images are the largest resource on most web pages, and they're the easiest place to save hundreds of kilobytes with almost no visible quality loss.

## Modern Formats

AVIF provides the best compression today — roughly 50% smaller than JPEG at the same perceptual quality. WebP is a safe fallback with broader support. Serve both and let the browser choose:

```html
<picture>
  <source type="image/avif" srcset="/hero.avif" />
  <source type="image/webp" srcset="/hero.webp" />
  <img src="/hero.jpg" alt="Hero image" width="1200" height="600" />
</picture>
```

## Responsive Sizes

Serving a 2400px image to a 375px viewport wastes bandwidth. Use `srcset` with multiple widths and `sizes` to tell the browser which one to pick:

```html
<img
  src="/hero-1200.jpg"
  srcset="/hero-400.jpg 400w, /hero-800.jpg 800w, /hero-1200.jpg 1200w"
  sizes="(max-width: 720px) 100vw, 1200px"
  width="1200"
  height="600"
  loading="lazy"
/>
```

## Automation

Do not hand-edit images. Use a build pipeline — Astro's built-in image optimization, Sharp, or a service like Cloudinary — that generates all formats and sizes from a single source. The build step adds a few seconds; the bandwidth savings last forever.

Astro's `<Image />` and `<Picture />` components handle this automatically. Use them instead of raw `<img>` tags wherever possible.

## The One Rule

Every image should have explicit `width` and `height` attributes. This prevents Cumulative Layout Shift and lets the browser reserve space before the image loads. It's the single highest-impact thing you can do for image performance.

```

---

## src/content/blog/markdown-style-guide.mdx

```md
---
title: 'Markdown Style Guide'
description: 'Here is a sample of some basic Markdown syntax that can be used when writing Markdown content in Astro.'
pubDate: '2024-06-19T00:00:00-04:00'
tags: ['general', 'css']
heroImage: '@images/markdown-style-guide-hero.webp'
---

Here is a sample of some basic Markdown syntax that can be used when writing Markdown content in Astro.

## Headings

The following HTML `<h1>`—`<h6>` elements represent six levels of section headings. `<h1>` is the highest section level while `<h6>` is the lowest.

# H1

## H2

### H3

#### H4

##### H5

###### H6

## Paragraph

Xerum, quo qui aut unt expliquam qui dolut labo. Aque venitatiusda cum, voluptionse latur sitiae dolessi aut parist aut dollo enim qui voluptate ma dolestendit peritin re plis aut quas inctum laceat est volestemque commosa as cus endigna tectur, offic to cor sequas etum rerum idem sintibus eiur? Quianimin porecus evelectur, cum que nis nust voloribus ratem aut omnimi, sitatur? Quiatem. Nam, omnis sum am facea corem alique molestrunt et eos evelece arcillit ut aut eos eos nus, sin conecerem erum fuga. Ri oditatquam, ad quibus unda veliamenimin cusam et facea ipsamus es exerum sitate dolores editium rerore eost, temped molorro ratiae volorro te reribus dolorer sperchicium faceata tiustia prat.

Itatur? Quiatae cullecum rem ent aut odis in re eossequodi nonsequ idebis ne sapicia is sinveli squiatum, core et que aut hariosam ex eat.

## Images

#### Syntax

```markdown
![Alt text](./full/or/relative/path/of/image)
```

#### Output

![blog placeholder](../../assets/images/blog-placeholder-about.jpg)

## Blockquotes

The blockquote element represents content that is quoted from another source, optionally with a citation which must be within a `footer` or `cite` element, and optionally with in-line changes such as annotations and abbreviations.

### Blockquote without attribution

#### Syntax

```markdown
> Tiam, ad mint andaepu dandae nostion secatur sequo quae.  
> **Note** that you can use _Markdown syntax_ within a blockquote.
```

#### Output

> Tiam, ad mint andaepu dandae nostion secatur sequo quae.  
> **Note** that you can use _Markdown syntax_ within a blockquote.

### Blockquote with attribution

#### Syntax

```markdown
> Don't communicate by sharing memory, share memory by communicating.<br>
> — <cite>Rob Pike[^1]</cite>
```

#### Output

> Don't communicate by sharing memory, share memory by communicating.<br />
> — <cite>Rob Pike[^1]</cite>

[^1]: The above quote is excerpted from Rob Pike's [talk](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PAAkCSZUG1c) during Gopherfest, November 18, 2015.

## Tables

#### Syntax

```markdown
| Italics   | Bold     | Code   |
| --------- | -------- | ------ |
| _italics_ | **bold** | `code` |
```

#### Output

| Italics   | Bold     | Code   |
| --------- | -------- | ------ |
| _italics_ | **bold** | `code` |

## Code Blocks

#### Syntax

we can use 3 backticks ``` in new line and write snippet and close with 3 backticks on new line and to highlight language specific syntax, write one word of language name after first 3 backticks, for eg. html, javascript, css, markdown, typescript, txt, bash

````markdown
```html
<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
  <head>
    <meta charset="utf-8" />
    <title>Example HTML5 Document</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <p>Test</p>
  </body>
</html>
```
````

#### Output

```html
<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
  <head>
    <meta charset="utf-8" />
    <title>Example HTML5 Document</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <p>Test</p>
  </body>
</html>
```

## List Types

### Ordered List

#### Syntax

```markdown
1. First item
2. Second item
3. Third item
```

#### Output

1. First item
2. Second item
3. Third item

### Unordered List

#### Syntax

```markdown
- List item
- Another item
- And another item
```

#### Output

- List item
- Another item
- And another item

### Nested list

#### Syntax

```markdown
- Fruit
  - Apple
  - Orange
  - Banana
- Dairy
  - Milk
  - Cheese
```

#### Output

- Fruit
  - Apple
  - Orange
  - Banana
- Dairy
  - Milk
  - Cheese

## Other Elements — abbr, sub, sup, kbd, mark

#### Syntax

```markdown
<abbr title="Graphics Interchange Format">GIF</abbr> is a bitmap image format.

H<sub>2</sub>O

X<sup>n</sup> + Y<sup>n</sup> = Z<sup>n</sup>

Press <kbd>CTRL</kbd> + <kbd>ALT</kbd> + <kbd>Delete</kbd> to end the session.

Most <mark>salamanders</mark> are nocturnal, and hunt for insects, worms, and other small creatures.
```

#### Output

<abbr title="Graphics Interchange Format">GIF</abbr> is a bitmap image format.

H<sub>2</sub>O

X<sup>n</sup> + Y<sup>n</sup> = Z<sup>n</sup>

Press <kbd>CTRL</kbd> + <kbd>ALT</kbd> + <kbd>Delete</kbd> to end the session.

Most <mark>salamanders</mark> are nocturnal, and hunt for insects, worms, and other small creatures.

```

---

## src/content/blog/modern-css-reset.mdx

```md
---
title: 'A Modern CSS Reset for 2024'
description: 'What a CSS reset looks like today — beyond margin-zero and box-sizing, with accessible defaults and reduced-motion guardrails built in.'
pubDate: '2022-09-16T00:00:00-04:00'
tags: ['css']
heroImage: '@images/modern-css-reset-hero.webp'
---

The classic reset — `* { margin: 0; box-sizing: border-box; }` — is a start, but it leaves too many inconsistencies on the table. Browsers have converged on defaults in the last few years, which means our resets can be more targeted.

## The Core

```css
*,
*::before,
*::after {
  box-sizing: border-box;
}

body {
  margin: 0;
  min-height: 100vh;
  line-height: 1.5;
  -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;
}

img, picture, video, canvas, svg {
  display: block;
  max-width: 100%;
}

input, button, textarea, select {
  font: inherit;
}
```

## Why These Matter

`line-height: 1.5` on the body sets a reasonable rhythm everywhere. `font: inherit` on form elements prevents them from defaulting to the browser's system font. Block display on images eliminates the mysterious 4px gap below them.

## Reduced Motion

Add a reduced-motion layer at the reset level so you don't have to remember it in every component:

```css
@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) {
  *,
  *::before,
  *::after {
    animation-duration: 0.01ms !important;
    animation-iteration-count: 1 !important;
    transition-duration: 0.01ms !important;
    scroll-behavior: auto !important;
  }
}
```

This isn't elegant, but it's effective. The `!important` is justified here — it's a user preference override.

```

---

## src/content/blog/personal-knowledge-management.mdx

```md
---
title: 'How I Organize My Technical Notes'
description: 'My system for capturing, linking, and retrieving engineering knowledge — plain text, git, and minimal friction.'
pubDate: '2022-11-11T00:00:00-05:00'
tags: ['meta']
heroImage: '@images/personal-knowledge-management-hero.webp'
---

After a decade of trying every note-taking app, I settled on plain Markdown files in a git repository. It's boring, portable, and searchable with tools that will outlive every SaaS product.

## The Structure

```
~/notes/
  /projects/          # One file per project
  /concepts/          # One file per technology or idea
  /log/               # Daily work logs
    2024/
      01-jan.md
      02-feb.md
  /references/        # Book notes, conference talks
  /dotfiles/          # Config snippets I always need
```

No nesting deeper than two levels. Deep hierarchies are where notes go to die.

## Linking Notes

I use `[[wiki-style links]]` within files — a simple grep finds all references to a concept across every note. No database needed. This is the most important habit: if a note mentions something that has its own file, link it.

## Daily Logs

Each workday gets a file. I timestamp significant events — a build that took too long, a bug that took three hours to find, a library that surprised me. These are not journal entries; they're evidence. When I encounter a similar problem six months later, I grep my logs.

## Search

`rg` (ripgrep) searches the entire repository in under a second. I alias `n` to search my notes directory:

```bash
alias n='rg --no-heading --smart-case ~/notes'
```

If I can't find it in three searches, I write a new note with better keywords.

## The Rule

If I've solved a problem twice, I write it down. The third time, I search before I start. That's the threshold where the system pays for itself.

```

---

## src/content/blog/react-server-components.mdx

```md
---
title: 'Understanding React Server Components'
description: 'What server components actually do, how they differ from SSR, and when they genuinely simplify your data-fetching architecture.'
pubDate: '2022-08-26T00:00:00-04:00'
tags: ['react']
heroImage: '@images/react-server-components-hero.webp'
---

React Server Components (RSC) are often explained as "components that run on the server," which is technically true but misses the point. SSR also runs components on the server. The difference is in the output: SSR sends HTML, RSC sends a serialized description of the component tree that the client can merge with its existing state.

## What Makes RSC Different

A Server Component can access the database directly, read from the filesystem, or call internal APIs — and none of that code ever reaches the client bundle.

```tsx
// This component runs only on the server.
// The SQL query, the data transformation, and the imports never ship to the browser.
async function RecentPosts() {
  const posts = await db.query('SELECT * FROM posts ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 5');
  return (
    <ul>
      {posts.map(post => (
        <li key={post.id}>{post.title}</li>
      ))}
    </ul>
  );
}
```

## The Client Boundary

The rule is simple: Server Components can import Client Components, but not the reverse. Add `'use client'` at the top of any file that needs interactivity, event handlers, or hooks like `useState` and `useEffect`.

This boundary is where most confusion lives. The solution is to push interactivity as far down the tree as possible, wrapping only the interactive parts in `'use client'` while keeping the rest server-rendered.

## When to Use RSC

RSC excels for data-heavy pages where most of the UI is static. Dashboards, product listing pages, and content-heavy sites benefit most. Highly interactive applications with constant state changes gain less.

## Performance Impact

The server component payload is more compact than the equivalent HTML because it's a binary format. Combined with streaming, this means the page starts rendering before all data is available — a meaningful improvement for slow database queries.

```

---

## src/content/blog/schema-org-microdata.mdx

```md
---
title: 'Schema.org Microdata on This Site'
description: 'A page-by-page inventory of what JSON-LD structured data is emitted, which entities appear, and how the schema changes with pageType.'
pubDate: 'Jun 17 2026'
tags: ['architecture', 'seo']
heroImage: '@images/schema-org-microdata-hero.webp'
---

Every page on this site emits a `<script type="application/ld+json">` block rendered by `SchemaOrg.astro`. The contents of that block vary by page type — a homepage gets different entities than an article or an about page.

This article documents every schema graph that ships to search engines, so you know exactly what structured data is available for indexing, rich results, and knowledge graph connections.

## The Author Entity (shared across all pages)

All page types include the author `Person` entity as the first item in the `@graph` array. It links back to the same `@id` everywhere so search engines resolve it as one identity:

```json
{
  "@type": "Person",
  "@id": "https://ericcarlisle.com/#person",
  "name": "Eric Carlisle",
  "url": "https://ericcarlisle.com",
  "image": {
    "@type": "ImageObject",
    "@id": "https://ericcarlisle.com/#personImage",
    "url": "https://ericcarlisle.com/eric-carlisle.webp"
  },
  "sameAs": [
    "https://linkedin.com/in/ericcarlisle",
    "https://github.com/ecarlisle"
  ],
  "jobTitle": "Principal Full-Stack Engineer & UX Architect",
  "worksFor": {
    "@type": "Organization",
    "name": "Eric Carlisle Consulting"
  }
}
```

## Homepage (`pageType="homepage"`)

The index page at `/` produces three entities in its `@graph`:

1. **`Person`** — author identity (see above).
2. **`WebSite`** — site-level metadata with a `SearchAction` potential action for Google's Sitelinks Search Box:
   ```json
   {
     "@type": "WebSite",
     "@id": "https://ericcarlisle.com/#website",
     "url": "https://ericcarlisle.com",
     "name": "Eric Carlisle",
     "publisher": { "@id": "https://ericcarlisle.com/#person" },
     "potentialAction": [{
       "@type": "SearchAction",
       "target": {
         "@type": "EntryPoint",
         "urlTemplate": "https://ericcarlisle.com/search?q={search_term_string}"
       },
       "query-input": "required name=search_term_string"
     }]
   }
   ```
3. **`CollectionPage`** — the page itself, linked to the website via `isPartOf`.

The `WebSite` entity only appears on the homepage — no other page type includes it. The `SearchAction` is how Google discovers your site search and can render a search box in search results.

## Blog Index & Paginated Pages (`pageType="collection"`)

Every collection page — including `/blog`, `/blog/page/2`, and the 404 page — emits a `CollectionPage` entity with a `BreadcrumbList`:

```json
{
  "@type": "BreadcrumbList",
  "@id": "https://ericcarlisle.com/blog/#breadcrumb",
  "itemListElement": [
    { "@type": "ListItem", "position": 1, "name": "Eric Carlisle", "item": "https://ericcarlisle.com" },
    { "@type": "ListItem", "position": 2, "name": "Blog", "item": "https://ericcarlisle.com/blog" }
  ]
}
```

When posts are passed to `BaseHead`, the `CollectionPage` also includes a `mainEntity`:

```json
"mainEntity": {
  "@type": "ItemList",
  "numberOfItems": 10,
  "itemListElement": [
    { "@type": "ListItem", "position": 1, "name": "...", "url": "..." }
  ]
}
```

The `collectionType` prop distinguishes three variants:

| collectionType | Breadcrumb | Notes |
|----------------|------------|-------|
| `main-feed` | Home → Blog | Standard blog index |
| `tag-filter` | Home → Tags → TagName | Filtered by tag |
| `tags-index` | Home → Tags | Tag archive overview |

The current pages that use `pageType="collection"` are:

| Route | collectionType | Has posts list? |
|-------|---------------|-----------------|
| `/blog` | `main-feed` (default) | Yes |
| `/blog/page/2` | `main-feed` (default) | Yes |
| `/404` | `main-feed` (default) | No (`robots: "noindex, follow"`) |

## Article Pages (`pageType="article"`)

Every blog post at `/blog/[...slug]/` produces a graph with:

1. **`Person`** — author.
2. **`WebPage`** — the page itself, with `mainEntity` pointing to the article.
3. **`BlogPosting`** — the full article schema.
4. **`BreadcrumbList`** — Home → Blog → Post Title.

The `BreadcrumbList` for articles has three items:

```json
{
  "@type": "BreadcrumbList",
  "@id": "https://ericcarlisle.com/blog/some-post/#breadcrumb",
  "itemListElement": [
    { "@type": "ListItem", "position": 1, "name": "Eric Carlisle", "item": "https://ericcarlisle.com" },
    { "@type": "ListItem", "position": 2, "name": "Blog", "item": "https://ericcarlisle.com/blog" },
    { "@type": "ListItem", "position": 3, "name": "Post Title", "item": "https://ericcarlisle.com/blog/some-post/" }
  ]
}
```

The `BlogPosting` entity drives search-result appearance — rich snippets, Google Discover, and knowledge panels. Key properties:

```json
{
  "@type": "BlogPosting",
  "@id": "https://ericcarlisle.com/blog/some-post/#article",
  "mainEntityOfPage": { "@id": "https://ericcarlisle.com/blog/some-post/#webpage" },
  "headline": "Post Title",
  "description": "Post description",
  "datePublished": "2026-06-17T00:00:00.000Z",
  "dateModified": "2026-06-17T00:00:00.000Z",
  "articleSection": "first-tag",
  "wordCount": 850,
  "timeRequired": "PT5M",
  "author": { "@type": "Person", "name": "Eric Carlisle", "@id": "https://ericcarlisle.com/#person" },
  "publisher": { "@type": "Person", "name": "Eric Carlisle", "@id": "https://ericcarlisle.com/#person" },
  "image": { "@type": "ImageObject", "@id": ".../#primaryimage", "url": "...", "width": 1200, "height": 630 },
  "inLanguage": "en-US"
}
```

Properties are conditionally included:

| Property | Condition |
|----------|-----------|
| `dateModified` | Only when `updatedDate` is set in frontmatter |
| `articleSection` | Only when `tags` has at least one entry |
| `wordCount`, `timeRequired` | Only when `wordCount > 0` |
| `image` | Always — falls back to `og-image.jpg` |

Reading time is computed as `Math.ceil(wordCount / 200)` and represented as ISO 8601 duration (`PT5M`).

Open Graph and Twitter meta tags also vary by article metadata:

| Tag | Source |
|-----|--------|
| `article:published_time` | `pubDate` |
| `article:modified_time` | `updatedDate` (optional) |
| `article:author` | "Eric Carlisle" |
| `article:section` | First tag |
| `article:tag` | Each tag (repeated per tag) |
| `og:type` | `article` |
| `twitter:creator` | `twitterHandle` from frontmatter (optional) |

## About & Contact Pages (`pageType="about"` / `pageType="contact"`)

These produce a graph with:

1. **`Person`** — author.
2. **`AboutPage`** or **`ContactPage`** — the page itself, with `mainEntity` pointing to `/#person`.
3. **`BreadcrumbList`** — Home → Page Title.

The `AboutPage` entity:

```json
{
  "@type": "AboutPage",
  "@id": "https://ericcarlisle.com/about/#webpage",
  "url": "https://ericcarlisle.com/about/",
  "name": "About Me",
  "description": "...",
  "mainEntity": { "@id": "https://ericcarlisle.com/#person" }
}
```

Pages that use these types:

| Route | pageType |
|-------|----------|
| `/about` | `about` |
| `/style-guide` | `about` (via BlogPost layout) |

## Page Type Summary

| pageType | Schema Types | Breadcrumb |
|----------|-------------|------------|
| `homepage` | `Person`, `WebSite`, `CollectionPage` | — |
| `collection` | `Person`, `CollectionPage`, `ItemList` | Home → Blog |
| `article` | `Person`, `WebPage`, `BlogPosting`, `BreadcrumbList` | Home → Blog → Post |
| `about` | `Person`, `AboutPage`, `BreadcrumbList` | Home → About |
| `contact` | `Person`, `ContactPage`, `BreadcrumbList` | Home → Contact |
| `portfolio` | `Person`, `WebPage`, `CreativeWork`, `BreadcrumbList` | Home → Portfolio |

All types also render Open Graph, Twitter Card, and standard meta tags via `BaseHead.astro`. The full `@graph` is rendered inside a single `<script type="application/ld+json">` element, placed just before `</head>`.

## Debugging Structured Data

To inspect the live schema on any page:

1. Open **Chrome DevTools → Elements** and search for `ld+json`.
2. Use **Google's [Rich Results Test](https://search.google.com/test/rich-results)** to validate the markup.
3. Use **Schema.org's [Validator](https://validator.schema.org/)** to trace `@id` references and verify entity relationships.

Every article page should pass the Rich Results Test with zero errors. If it doesn't, the most common cause is a missing or invalid `heroImage` path, since `image` is always included in the `BlogPosting` entity.

```

---

## src/content/blog/static-site-search.mdx

```md
---
title: 'Adding Search to a Static Site Without a Backend'
description: 'Client-side search strategies — Lunr, Fuse.js, Pagefind, and when each makes sense for a content site with no server component.'
pubDate: '2022-10-21T00:00:00-04:00'
tags: ['architecture', 'engineering']
heroImage: '@images/static-site-search-hero.webp'
---

Static sites don't have a server to run a search index against, so search has to happen in the browser. The trade-off is index size versus search quality.

## Pagefind

Pagefind is the current best answer for most sites. It generates a search index at build time and provides a lean client-side bundle for querying it.

```bash
npx pagefind --site dist
```

The index is fractionated and compressed — a 1000-page site generates roughly 300 KB of index data, loaded incrementally as the user types. It supports fuzzy matching, filtering by metadata, and custom result UI.

## Lunr

Lunr is a client-side search library that builds its index in the browser. It works entirely offline and supports field-scoped queries (title vs body), but the entire index must be downloaded before any search works. For small sites (under 50 pages) this is fine. For larger sites, the initial load cost is noticeable.

## Fuse.js

Fuse.js is the lightest option at about 8 KB. It does simple pattern matching with configurable fuzziness. It's good for filtering UI elements — dropdowns, tag selectors, command palettes — but doesn't support relevance scoring well enough for full-text search on a content site.

## What Not to Do

Don't embed Google Custom Search. It loads ads, tracks users, and the results styling is nearly impossible to match to your site's design. Algolia DocSearch is a good alternative if you're willing to depend on a third-party service, but for a personal blog, Pagefind gives you the same experience with zero external dependencies.

```

---

## src/content/blog/typescript-react-patterns.mdx

```md
---
title: 'TypeScript Patterns for React Components'
description: 'Generic components, discriminated union props, inference patterns, and when to reach for forwardRef in production React code.'
pubDate: '2022-09-23T00:00:00-04:00'
tags: ['typescript', 'react']
heroImage: '@images/typescript-react-patterns-hero.webp'
---

TypeScript with React can be either a joy or a constant fight. The difference usually comes down to a few patterns that handle the awkward edges well.

## Generic Components

When a component accepts a value and an onChange, infer the type from the props:

```tsx
type Option<T extends string> = { label: string; value: T };

function Select<T extends string>({ options, value, onChange }: {
  options: Option<T>[];
  value: T;
  onChange: (value: T) => void;
}) {
  return (
    <select value={value} onChange={e => onChange(e.target.value as T)}>
      {options.map(o => (
        <option key={o.value} value={o.value}>{o.label}</option>
      ))}
    </select>
  );
}
```

Now `onChange` receives the exact union type of your options, not a generic `string`.

## Discriminated Union Props

Use a `type` discriminant to enforce mutually exclusive prop combinations:

```tsx
type ButtonProps = {
  label: string;
} & (
  | { variant: 'primary'; action: () => void }
  | { variant: 'link'; href: string; external?: boolean }
);
```

TypeScript enforces that you provide `action` with primary and `href` with link. Impossible states are impossible to represent.

## When Not to Use forwardRef

If your component is a simple wrapper around a native element, use `forwardRef` so parent components can access the DOM node. If your component manages internal state and doesn't expose a DOM-facing API, skip it. The ergonomic cost of `forwardRef` with generic types is higher than most people admit.

```

---

## src/content/blog/web-accessibility-auditing.mdx

```md
---
title: 'Web Accessibility Auditing: A Practical Guide'
description: 'Automated scanners, manual testing checklists, screen reader workflows, and how to prioritize findings that actually impact users.'
pubDate: '2022-10-07T00:00:00-04:00'
tags: ['accessibility', 'engineering']
heroImage: '@images/web-accessibility-auditing-hero.webp'
---

An accessibility audit isn't a one-time quality gate — it's a diagnostic that tells you where your product fails for people with disabilities. The goal is a prioritized list of fixes, not a score.

## Automated Scanning

Tools like axe DevTools, WAVE, and Lighthouse catch roughly 30% of accessibility issues. They're excellent at finding missing alt text, insufficient color contrast, and missing form labels. Run them in CI alongside your linter.

But automated tools can't tell you if a focus order makes sense or if a screen reader announcement is confusing. That's where manual testing comes in.

## Manual Testing Checklist

1. Navigate the entire page with Tab and Shift+Tab. Is every interactive element reachable? Do you ever lose focus or get trapped?
2. Turn on a screen reader (VoiceOver on Mac, NVDA on Windows). Navigate by heading, by landmark, and by tab. Does the page structure make sense?
3. Zoom to 200%. Is everything still readable without horizontal scrolling?
4. Disable images. Is all essential information available as text alternatives?

## Prioritization

- **P0**: Blocks a critical user flow (can't submit a form with a screen reader)
- **P1**: Makes a flow significantly harder (poor focus order, unclear announcements)
- **P2**: Violates best practices but has a workaround (missing ARIA labels on decorative elements)

Fix P0 issues immediately. Schedule P1 for the next sprint. Track P2 in the backlog.

## Building an Accessibility Culture

The audit is only as good as the follow-through. Assign owners to each finding, link to the specific WCAG criterion, and verify the fix by running the failing check again. Accessibility is a habit, not a milestone.

```

---

## src/content/blog/web-performance-budgets.mdx

```md
---
title: 'Setting a Web Performance Budget'
description: 'How to define, measure, and enforce performance budgets that keep your site fast without slowing down development.'
pubDate: '2022-07-29T00:00:00-04:00'
tags: ['performance']
heroImage: '@images/web-performance-budgets-hero.webp'
---

Performance budgets are the guardrails that keep a project from gradually slowing down. Without them, every new feature, library, or image adds a little more weight, and before you know it, your bundle has doubled.

This isn't about arbitrary limits. A good budget is derived from real data — your users' devices, network conditions, and business metrics.

## Choosing Your Metrics

Start with metrics you can measure consistently. Lighthouse scores are a good conversation starter, but for CI enforcement, use raw timing metrics:

- **LCP** should stay under 2.5s on the 75th percentile
- **Total bundle JS** under 150 KB gzipped for initial route
- **Total image weight** under 500 KB per page

These aren't universal. If your audience is mostly mobile users on 3G in India, your thresholds need to be tighter than if they're desktop users on fiber in North America.

## Tools for Enforcement

Lighthouse CI integrates into your CI pipeline and fails a build when a budget is exceeded. Bundle Buddy and Webpack Bundle Analyzer give you per-component breakdowns so you can see exactly which import pushed you over.

The key is to make the failure visible. A passing build that's 5 KB over budget is still over budget. Every regression needs a ticket.

## When to Relax a Budget

Budgets should be updated when you add a genuinely valuable feature, not when you're sloppy. The process: propose the increase, justify it with the feature's value, and look for something else to cut first.

This creates a culture of performance consciousness. Every team member thinks twice before adding that 50 KB dependency.

## TL;DR

Set thresholds based on real user data, enforce them in CI, and treat every overage as a conscious trade-off rather than an accident.

```

---

## src/content/blog/working-with-id-without-losing-ourselves.mdx

```md
---
title: Working With AI Without Losing Ourselves
description: 'How to collaborate with AI without losing our sense of self. Why we anthropomorphize it, where that helps, where it hurts, and how to stay grounded.'
draft: true
pubDate: 2026-06-20T00:00:00-04:00
tags: ['AI']

---
# COPILOT : DON'T OFFER HELP WITH THIS FILE!!!
Recently, I had an interview with an AI. It was... it was new.

I accepted the interview with honest enthusiasm mixed with a healthy dose of skepticism. The AI 
had a video avata

I started the interview 


1. Sign in at webmention.io (https://webmention.io) with your GitHub account — it'll verify ownership via the <link rel="me" /> we just added. Grab your API token from the settings page.
2. Set the token in .env: WEBMENTION_IO_TOKEN=your_token_here
3. Connect brid.gy (https://brid.gy) — sign in with your Bluesky and Mastodon accounts. It'll forward replies/likes/reposts from those platforms as webmentions to your inbox.
Once those are done, run pnpm build and mentions will be baked into the static HTML. Rebuild periodically (via cron, deploy hook, etc.) to pull in new mentions.

```

## src/layouts/BlogPost.astro

```astro
---
import { Image } from 'astro:assets';
import BaseHead from '@components/BaseHead.astro';
import Footer from '@components/Footer.astro';
import FormattedDate from '@components/FormattedDate.astro';
import Header from '@components/Header.astro';
import PaginationNav from '@components/PaginationNav.astro';
import ShareStrip from '@components/ShareStrip.astro';
import TagFilterBar from '@components/TagFilterBar.astro';
import Webmentions from '@components/Webmentions.astro';
import type { TagData } from '@lib/blog-utils';
import type { Webmention } from '@lib/webmentions';
import type { ImageMetadata } from 'astro';

interface Props {
  title: string;
  description: string;
  pageType?: 'article' | 'about' | 'contact' | 'portfolio';
  pubDate?: Date;
  updatedDate?: Date;
  heroImage?: ImageMetadata;
  coverAlt?: string;
  tags?: string[];
  socialTitle?: string;
  socialDescription?: string;
  socialImage?: ImageMetadata;
  twitterHandle?: string;
  share?: { enabled?: boolean; networks?: string[]; scheduledFor?: Date };
  wordCount?: number;
  prevPost?: { title: string; slug: string };
  nextPost?: { title: string; slug: string };
  tagData?: TagData;
  webmentions?: Webmention[];
}

const {
  title,
  description,
  pubDate,
  updatedDate,
  heroImage,
  coverAlt,
  tags,
  socialTitle,
  socialDescription,
  socialImage,
  twitterHandle,
  wordCount = 0,
  pageType = 'article',
  prevPost,
  nextPost,
  share,
  tagData,
  webmentions,
} = Astro.props;

const readingTimeMin = Math.ceil(wordCount / 200);
const pageUrl = new URL(Astro.url.pathname, Astro.site).href;
---

<html lang="en">
	<head>
		<BaseHead
      {title}
      {description}
      {socialTitle}
      {socialDescription}
      image={socialImage ?? heroImage}
      {pageType}
      {pubDate}
      {updatedDate}
      {tags}
      {wordCount}
      {twitterHandle}
    />
	<style>
		.hero-image {
			width: 100%;
      max-width: 1020px;
      margin: 0 auto var(--space-section);
		}

		.hero-image img {
			display: block;
			margin: 0 auto;
			border-radius: var(--radius-lg);
		}

		.post-layout {
			display: grid;
			grid-template-columns: 1fr;
			gap: var(--space-component-lg);
      position: relative;
		}

		@media (min-width: 1024px) {
			.post-layout {
				grid-template-columns: 1fr 220px;
			}

			.post-layout .post-content {
				grid-column: 1;
        min-width: 0;
			}

			.post-layout .post-sidebar {
				grid-column: 2;
			}

			.post-sidebar-inner {
				position: sticky;
				top: var(--space-component-lg);
			}

			.sidebar-share {
				padding: var(--space-component) 0 var(--space-component);
				margin-bottom: var(--space-component);
				border-bottom: var(--size-border-pixel) solid var(--border-main);
			}

			.sidebar-share :global(.share) {
				padding: 0;
				justify-content: flex-start;
			}
		}

		.prose {
			padding: var(--space-component);
		}

    .page-header {
      max-width: var(--layout-max-width);
      margin: 0 auto var(--space-component-lg);
      padding-top: var(--space-component);
    }

    .page-header--article {
      max-width: 1020px;
    }

    .page-header h1 {
      max-width: 20ch;
    }

		.date {
			margin-bottom: var(--space-xs);
			color: var(--text-muted);
      display: flex;
      align-items: center;
      justify-content: flex-start;
      flex-wrap: wrap;
      gap: 0;
      font-family: var(--font-mono);
		}

		.last-updated-on {
			font-style: italic;
      width: 100%;
		}

    .article-tags {
      margin-top: var(--space-sm);
      text-align: center;
    }
	</style>
	</head>

	<body>
    {pageType === 'article' && (
      <div class="reading-progress" id="reading-progress" role="progressbar" aria-valuenow="0" aria-valuemin="0" aria-valuemax="100" aria-label="Reading progress"></div>
    )}
		<a href="#main-content" class="skip-link">Skip to main content</a>
		<Header />
		<main id="main-content" tabindex="-1">
      {pageType === 'article' && (
        <a href="/blog" class="back-link">&larr; Back to all posts</a>
      )}
			<article>
        <header
          class:list={[
            'page-header',
            pageType === 'article' ? 'page-header--article' : 'page-header--standard',
          ]}
        >
          {pageType === 'article' && pubDate && (
            <div class="date">
              <FormattedDate date={pubDate} />
              {readingTimeMin > 0 && <span class="reading-time">{readingTimeMin} min read</span>}
              {updatedDate && (
                <div class="last-updated-on">
                  Last updated on <FormattedDate date={updatedDate} />
                </div>
              )}
            </div>
          )}
          <h1>{title}</h1>
          <hr />
        </header>
        {heroImage && (
          <div class="hero-image">
            <Image
              width={1020}
              height={510}
              src={heroImage}
              alt={coverAlt || title}
              widths={[510, 765, 1020]}
              sizes="(min-width: 1200px) 1020px, (min-width: 720px) calc(100vw - 3.5rem), calc(100vw - 3rem)"
            />
          </div>
        )}
				<div class="post-layout">
					<div class="post-content">
						<div class="prose">
							<slot />
						</div>

            {pageType === 'article' && tags && tags.length > 0 && (
              <div class="prose article-tags">
                <TagFilterBar tags={tags} tagData={tagData} />
              </div>
            )}

            {pageType === 'article' && (prevPost || nextPost) && (
              <PaginationNav
                prev={prevPost ? { url: `/blog/${prevPost.slug}/`, label: '← Previous', title: prevPost.title } : undefined}
                next={nextPost ? { url: `/blog/${nextPost.slug}/`, label: 'Next →', title: nextPost.title } : undefined}
              />
            )}

            {pageType === 'article' && webmentions && (
              <Webmentions mentions={webmentions} />
            )}
					</div>

          {pageType === 'article' && (
            <aside class="post-sidebar">
              <div class="post-sidebar-inner">
                {share?.enabled !== false && (
                  <div class="sidebar-share">
                    <ShareStrip {title} {pageUrl} />
                  </div>
                )}
                <div class="toc" id="table-of-contents">
                  <h3>On this page</h3>
                  <nav aria-label="Table of contents">
                    <ul id="toc-list"></ul>
                  </nav>
                </div>
              </div>
            </aside>
          )}
				</div>
			</article>
		</main>
		<Footer />
		<pagefind-modal></pagefind-modal>
	</body>
</html>

<script>
  const progressBar = document.getElementById('reading-progress') as HTMLDivElement;

  function updateProgress(): void {
    if (!progressBar) return;
    const scrollTop = window.scrollY;
    const docHeight = document.documentElement.scrollHeight - window.innerHeight;
    const progress = docHeight > 0 ? Math.min((scrollTop / docHeight) * 100, 100) : 0;
    progressBar.style.width = `${progress}%`;
    progressBar.setAttribute('aria-valuenow', String(Math.round(progress)));
  }

  if (progressBar) {
    window.addEventListener('scroll', updateProgress, { passive: true });
    window.addEventListener('resize', updateProgress, { passive: true });
    updateProgress();
  }

  const article = document.querySelector('article');
  const tocList = document.getElementById('toc-list');
  if (article && tocList) {
    const headings = article.querySelectorAll('.post-content h2, .post-content h3');
    const items: Array<{ id: string; text: string; level: number }> = [];

    headings.forEach((heading) => {
      if (!heading.id) {
        heading.id =
          heading.textContent
            ?.toLowerCase()
            .replace(/[^a-z0-9]+/g, '-')
            .replace(/^-|-$/g, '') || '';
      }
      items.push({
        id: heading.id,
        text: heading.textContent || '',
        level: heading.tagName === 'H2' ? 2 : 3,
      });
    });

    if (items.length > 0) {
      items.forEach((item) => {
        const li = document.createElement('li');
        const link = document.createElement('a');
        link.href = `#${item.id}`;
        link.textContent = item.text;
        if (item.level === 3) {
          link.style.paddingLeft = 'var(--space-component)';
        }
        li.appendChild(link);
        tocList.appendChild(li);
      });

      const tocLinks = tocList.querySelectorAll('a');
      function updateActiveToc(): void {
        let currentId = '';
        items.forEach((item) => {
          const element = document.getElementById(item.id);
          if (element && element.getBoundingClientRect().top < 100) {
            currentId = item.id;
          }
        });
        tocLinks.forEach((link) => {
          link.classList.toggle('active', link.getAttribute('href') === `#${currentId}`);
        });
      }
      window.addEventListener('scroll', updateActiveToc, { passive: true });
      updateActiveToc();
    } else {
      const toc = document.getElementById('table-of-contents') as HTMLElement;
      toc.style.display = 'none';
    }
  }
</script>

```

## src/lib/blog-utils.ts

```ts
import type { CollectionEntry } from 'astro:content';

export const PAGE_SIZE = 10;

export interface TagData {
  allTags: string[];
  tagCounts: Record<string, number>;
}

export function computeTagData(allPosts: CollectionEntry<'blog'>[]): TagData {
  const allTags = [...new Set(allPosts.flatMap((p) => p.data.tags ?? []))].sort();

  const tagCounts: Record<string, number> = {};

  allPosts.forEach((post) => {
    for (const tag of post.data.tags ?? []) {
      tagCounts[tag] = (tagCounts[tag] ?? 0) + 1;
    }
  });

  return { allTags, tagCounts };
}

export async function getSortedPosts(): Promise<CollectionEntry<'blog'>[]> {
  const { getCollection } = await import('astro:content');
  const allPosts = await getCollection('blog', ({ data }) => data.draft !== true);
  return allPosts.toSorted((a, b) => b.data.pubDate.valueOf() - a.data.pubDate.valueOf());
}

```

## src/lib/webmentions.ts

```ts
export interface WebmentionAuthor {
  name?: string;
  photo?: string;
  url?: string;
}

export interface WebmentionContent {
  text?: string;
  html?: string;
}

export interface Webmention {
  type: string;
  author?: WebmentionAuthor;
  url?: string;
  published?: string;
  'wm-received'?: string;
  'wm-id': number;
  'wm-source': string;
  'wm-target': string;
  'wm-property': 'in-reply-to' | 'like-of' | 'repost-of' | 'mention-of' | 'bookmark-of';
  'wm-private'?: boolean;
  content?: WebmentionContent;
  name?: string;
}

interface WebmentionFeed {
  type: string;
  name: string;
  children: Webmention[];
}

const WEBMENTION_IO_DOMAIN = 'ericcarlisle.com';

function buildMentionsApiUrl(token: string, targetUrl: string): string {
  const url = new URL('https://webmention.io/api/mentions.jf2');
  url.searchParams.set('domain', WEBMENTION_IO_DOMAIN);
  url.searchParams.set('token', token);
  url.searchParams.set('target', targetUrl);
  url.searchParams.set('per-page', '50');
  url.searchParams.set('sort-by', 'published');
  return url.toString();
}

async function fetchFromWebmentionIo(apiUrl: string, targetUrl: string): Promise<Webmention[]> {
  try {
    const res = await fetch(apiUrl);
    if (!res.ok) {
      console.warn(`webmention.io returned ${res.status} for ${targetUrl}`);
      return [];
    }
    const feed: WebmentionFeed = await res.json();
    return feed.children ?? [];
  } catch (e) {
    console.warn(`Failed to fetch webmentions for ${targetUrl}:`, e);
    return [];
  }
}

function handleMissingToken(): Webmention[] {
  if (import.meta.env.DEV) {
    console.warn('WEBMENTION_IO_TOKEN is not set — returning mock mentions for local dev');
    return getMockWebmentions();
  }
  console.warn('WEBMENTION_IO_TOKEN is not set — skipping webmention fetch');
  return [];
}

export async function fetchWebmentions(targetUrl: string): Promise<Webmention[]> {
  const token = import.meta.env.WEBMENTION_IO_TOKEN;
  if (!token) {
    return handleMissingToken();
  }

  const apiUrl = buildMentionsApiUrl(token, targetUrl);
  return fetchFromWebmentionIo(apiUrl, targetUrl);
}

const MOCK_WEBMENTIONS: Webmention[] = [
  {
    type: 'entry',
    author: {
      name: 'Sarah Chen',
      photo: 'https://api.dicebear.com/7.x/avataaars/svg?seed=sarah',
      url: 'https://example.com/sarah',
    },
    url: 'https://example.com/sarah#reply-1',
    'wm-id': 1001,
    'wm-source': 'https://example.com/sarah',
    'wm-target': 'https://ericcarlisle.com/blog/test/',
    'wm-property': 'in-reply-to',
    published: new Date(Date.now() - 3600000).toISOString(),
    content: {
      html: "<p>Great breakdown of the approach. I especially liked the section on build-time data fetching — that's exactly the pattern I was looking for.</p>",
    },
  },
  {
    type: 'entry',
    author: {
      name: 'Marcus Rivera',
      photo: 'https://api.dicebear.com/7.x/avataaars/svg?seed=marcus',
      url: 'https://example.com/marcus',
    },
    url: 'https://example.com/marcus#reply-2',
    'wm-id': 1002,
    'wm-source': 'https://example.com/marcus',
    'wm-target': 'https://ericcarlisle.com/blog/test/',
    'wm-property': 'in-reply-to',
    published: new Date(Date.now() - 7200000).toISOString(),
    content: {
      html: "<p>Have you considered using a cron job to rebuild automatically when new mentions come in? That's what I do on my static site.</p>",
    },
  },
  {
    type: 'entry',
    author: {
      name: 'Aiko Tanaka',
      photo: 'https://api.dicebear.com/7.x/avataaars/svg?seed=aiko',
      url: 'https://example.com/aiko',
    },
    url: 'https://example.com/aiko',
    'wm-id': 1003,
    'wm-source': 'https://example.com/aiko',
    'wm-target': 'https://ericcarlisle.com/blog/test/',
    'wm-property': 'like-of',
    published: new Date(Date.now() - 86400000).toISOString(),
  },
  {
    type: 'entry',
    author: {
      name: 'Jamie Patel',
      photo: 'https://api.dicebear.com/7.x/avataaars/svg?seed=jamie',
      url: 'https://example.com/jamie',
    },
    url: 'https://example.com/jamie',
    'wm-id': 1004,

];

function getMockWebmentions(): Webmention[] {
  return MOCK_WEBMENTIONS;
}

## src/lib/word-count.ts

```ts
import type { CollectionEntry } from 'astro:content';

function countWords(text: string): number {
  const cleaned = text
    .replace(/^---[\s\S]*?---/, '')
    .replace(/<[^>]*>/g, '')
    .replace(/```[\s\S]*?```/g, '')
    .trim();
  return cleaned ? cleaned.split(/\s+/).filter(Boolean).length : 0;
}

export function getPostWordCount(post: CollectionEntry<'blog'>): number {
  return countWords(post.body ?? '');
}

export function getTotalWordCount(posts: CollectionEntry<'blog'>[]): number {
  return posts.reduce((sum, p) => sum + getPostWordCount(p), 0);
}

export function formatWordCount(count: number): string {
  return count >= 1000 ? `${(count / 1000).toFixed(0)}K` : `${count}`;
}

```

## src/pages/404.astro

```astro
---
import BaseHead from '@components/BaseHead.astro';
import Footer from '@components/Footer.astro';
import Header from '@components/Header.astro';
import { SITE_TITLE } from '../consts';
---

<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
  <head>
    <BaseHead title={`404 — ${SITE_TITLE}`} description="Page not found." pageType="collection" robots="noindex" />
  </head>
  <body>
    <a href="#main-content" class="skip-link">Skip to main content</a>
    <Header />
    <main id="main-content" tabindex="-1">
      <section class="not-found">
        <h1 class="not-found-code" aria-hidden="true">404</h1>
        <p class="not-found-label">Page not found</p>
        <p>This page wandered off. We suspect it joined a startup and is now building a competitor.</p>
        <a href="/" class="button">Back to home</a>
      </section>
    </main>
    <Footer />
    <pagefind-modal></pagefind-modal>
  </body>
</html>

<style>
  .not-found {
    display: flex;
    flex-direction: column;
    align-items: center;
    justify-content: center;
    min-height: 60vh;
    text-align: center;
    gap: var(--space-component);
    padding: var(--space-section) var(--space-component);
  }

  .not-found-code {
    font-family: var(--font-mono);
    font-size: clamp(4rem, 12vw, 8rem);
    line-height: 1;
    color: var(--color-action);
    margin: 0;
    animation: fade-up 0.6s ease-out;
  }

  .not-found-label {
    font-size: var(--type-size-large);
    color: var(--text-secondary);
    margin: 0;
    text-transform: uppercase;
    letter-spacing: 0.08em;
  }

  .not-found p {
    max-width: 36ch;
    color: var(--text-secondary);
    margin: 0;
  }

  .button {
    margin-top: var(--space-component);
  }
</style>

```

## src/pages/about.astro

```astro
---
import Layout from '../layouts/BlogPost.astro';
---

<Layout
	title="About Me"
	description="Principal Full-Stack Engineer & UX Architect building high-performance web experiences."
	pageType="about"
>
	<div class="stats-grid">
		<div class="stat">
			<span class="stat-value">15+</span>
			<span class="stat-label">Years Experience</span>
		</div>
		<div class="stat">
			<span class="stat-value">40+</span>
			<span class="stat-label">Systems Shipped</span>
		</div>
		<div class="stat">
			<span class="stat-value">99.9</span>
			<span class="stat-label">SLA P99</span>
		</div>
		<div class="stat">
			<span class="stat-value">12</span>
			<span class="stat-label">Open Source</span>
		</div>
	</div>

	<p>
		I'm a principal full-stack engineer and UX architect with over a decade of experience building
		high-performance web applications. I specialize in front-end architecture, design systems,
		accessibility, and modern CSS — the kind of work that makes interfaces feel intentional rather
		than assembled.
	</p>

	<p>
		I've spent my career straddling the line between engineering and design. That means I think in
		terms of systems, not pages: component libraries, design tokens, automated visual regression,
		custom Eleventy and Astro setups, performance budgets, and CI pipelines that catch regressions
		before they ship. I care about load times, color contrast ratios, keyboard flows, and what
		happens when a screen reader encounters a dynamically updated region.
	</p>

	<p>
		I'm also the principal behind <strong>Eric Carlisle Consulting</strong>, where I partner with
		teams to improve their front-end infrastructure — auditing existing codebases, establishing
		design systems, introducing accessibility practices, and mentoring developers who want to level
		up their craft. Every engagement is different, but the goal is always the same: build
		experiences that are fast, durable, and usable by everyone.
	</p>

	<p>
		This blog is where I write about what I'm learning. You'll find deep dives into web
		performance and Core Web Vitals, practical guides on Astro, React, and CSS architecture,
		notes on accessibility patterns I've encountered in the wild, and reflections on systems
		design — both in software and in the teams that build it.
	</p>

	<p>
		When I'm not at a keyboard, I'm usually reading, hiking, or tinkering with something that
		has no deadline and no delivery date. If any of this resonates, I'd love to hear from you.
		The best way to reach me is through <a href="https://linkedin.com/in/ericcarlisle">LinkedIn</a>
		or <a href="https://github.com/ecarlisle">GitHub</a>.
	</p>
</Layout>

```

## src/pages/blog.astro

```astro
---
import BaseHead from '@components/BaseHead.astro';
import Footer from '@components/Footer.astro';
import Header from '@components/Header.astro';
import PaginationNav from '@components/PaginationNav.astro';
import PostGrid from '@components/PostGrid.astro';
import TagFilterBar from '@components/TagFilterBar.astro';
import '@styles/feed.css';
import { computeTagData, getSortedPosts, PAGE_SIZE } from '@lib/blog-utils';
import { AUTHOR_NAME, SITE_TITLE } from '../consts';

const allPosts = await getSortedPosts();
const posts = allPosts.slice(0, PAGE_SIZE);
const totalPages = Math.ceil(allPosts.length / PAGE_SIZE);

const tagData = computeTagData(allPosts);
---

<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
  <head>
    <BaseHead title={`Blog — ${SITE_TITLE}`} description="All blog posts" pageType="collection" />
  </head>
  <body>
    <a href="#main-content" class="skip-link">Skip to main content</a>
    <Header />
    <main id="main-content" tabindex="-1">
      <section class="feed">
        <header class="collection-header">
          <h1>Blog</h1>
          <p class="collection-description">All posts ({allPosts.length} total)</p>
        </header>

        <TagFilterBar tagData={tagData} />

        <PostGrid posts={posts} author={AUTHOR_NAME} />

        {totalPages > 1 && (
          <PaginationNav next={{ url: '/blog/page/2', label: 'Next page →' }} />
        )}
      </section>
    </main>
    <Footer />
    <pagefind-modal></pagefind-modal>
  </body>
</html>

```
